# houstonbankruptcy.org -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on houstonbankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).

## The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/341-meeting.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›341 Meeting

The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Houston

The 341 meeting is the one required appearance in most bankruptcy cases. Here is what Houston filers need to know.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

Section 341 of the Bankruptcy Code requires every debtor to appear and answer questions under oath. The meeting is conducted by the trustee - not a judge. Its purpose is to verify the information in your petition.

Despite its name ("meeting of creditors"), creditors rarely attend in consumer cases.

5-10 min

Typical duration

21-40 days

After filing

1

Appearance required

Common Questions Asked

Did you review your petition before it was filed?

Is the information true and correct?

Did you list all of your assets?

Did you list all of your debts?

Have you sold, transferred, or given away property in the last 2-4 years?

Are you expecting inheritance, lawsuit proceeds, or tax refunds?

Have you filed bankruptcy before?

Are you current on tax filings?

Do you have domestic support obligations?

Is there anything you want to change in your petition?

How to Prepare

What to Bring

Valid government-issued photo ID

Proof of Social Security number (SSN card, W-2, or tax return)

Any documents your attorney requests

How to Act

Answer honestly and briefly - do not volunteer extra information

If you do not know, say so rather than guessing

Let your attorney handle disputes or complex questions

Dress neatly; formal attire is not required

S.D. Tex. Specifics

In the S.D. Tex., 341 meetings are typically held at or near 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. Many are conducted by telephone or video conference since 2020.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A required hearing where you answer questions under oath about your finances. In the S.D. Tex., most are conducted by phone or video.

What questions will the trustee ask?

Common questions: Is your petition true and correct? Did you list all assets and debts? Have you transferred property recently? Do you expect inheritance or lawsuit proceeds?

Where is the 341 meeting held in Houston?

Typically at or near 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. Many are now by phone or video conference.

Do creditors actually show up?

In most consumer cases, no. Usually just you, your attorney, and the trustee.

How long does it last?

5-10 minutes typically. The trustee asks standard questions and reviews documents.

What should I bring?

Valid photo ID, proof of Social Security number (SSN card, W-2, or tax return), and any documents your attorney requests.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## The Automatic Stay in Houston Bankruptcy
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/automatic-stay.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Automatic Stay

The Automatic Stay in Houston Bankruptcy

The moment you file bankruptcy in the S.D. Tex., a powerful federal court order called the automatic stay takes effect. Here is what it means for Houston residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is the Automatic Stay?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 362, the automatic stay is an injunction that immediately halts nearly all collection efforts against you the instant your bankruptcy petition is filed with the S.D. Tex.. It applies to all creditors, whether they know about the filing or not.

For Houston residents, this means that foreclosure proceedings, wage garnishment, vehicle repossession, debt collection lawsuits, harassing phone calls, bank levies, and utility shutoffs must stop immediately.

What the Automatic Stay Stops

Foreclosure

Non-Judicial sales halted

Garnishment

Wage deductions stopped

Repossession

Vehicle seizure blocked

Lawsuits

Collection suits frozen

Foreclosure: Texas uses non-judicial foreclosure. The automatic stay halts the process regardless of how far along it is.

Wage garnishment: Wages cannot be garnished for consumer debt in TX. The automatic stay stops all garnishment that is in progress.

Vehicle repossession: Creditors cannot repossess your car after you file. If your car was recently repossessed, you may be able to recover it.

Collection lawsuits: All pending lawsuits for debt collection are stayed.

Phone calls and letters: Debt collectors must stop all contact immediately.

Bank levies: Creditors cannot freeze or seize your bank accounts.

Utility shutoffs: Utilities cannot disconnect service for 20 days after filing.

What the Automatic Stay Does NOT Stop

Child support and alimony: Domestic support obligations continue unaffected.

Criminal proceedings: The stay does not halt criminal cases.

Certain tax proceedings: Tax audits and tax court proceedings may continue.

Prior eviction judgments: If the landlord already obtained a judgment for possession, the eviction may proceed.

Pension loan repayment: Payroll deductions for retirement plan loans continue.

Stay Violations in the S.D. Tex.

If a creditor continues collection activity after you file bankruptcy, they are violating the automatic stay. Under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k), you may be entitled to:

Actual damages - any financial harm caused by the violation

Attorney fees and costs - the cost of enforcing the stay

Punitive damages - in cases of willful violation

Document every violation carefully: save voicemails, letters, garnishment notices, and any evidence of continued collection.

Repeat Filers: Limited Stay Protection

One dismissal in the past year: The stay lasts only 30 days unless you file a motion to extend it and show good faith (section 362(c)(3)).

Two or more dismissals in the past year: The stay does not go into effect at all unless you file a motion to impose it (section 362(c)(4)).

If you are a repeat filer, timing your petition and filing the appropriate motions immediately is critical.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the automatic stay in bankruptcy?

The automatic stay is a federal court order under 11 U.S.C. section 362 that takes effect the instant you file bankruptcy in the S.D. Tex.. It immediately stops most collection actions including wage garnishment, foreclosure proceedings, repossession attempts, lawsuits, harassing phone calls, and utility shutoffs.

What does the automatic stay stop?

The automatic stay stops: wage garnishment, foreclosure sales, vehicle repossession, debt collection lawsuits, creditor phone calls and letters, bank account levies, utility disconnection (for 20 days), eviction (in some cases), and IRS collection activity.

What does the automatic stay NOT stop?

The automatic stay does not stop: child support and alimony collection (domestic support obligations), most criminal proceedings, certain tax audit proceedings, and evictions where the landlord already had a judgment for possession before filing.

What happens if a creditor violates the automatic stay in Houston?

If a creditor violates the automatic stay, you may be entitled to actual damages, attorney fees, and in some cases punitive damages under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k). Document the violation and notify your attorney immediately. The S.D. Tex. takes stay violations seriously.

Does the automatic stay apply if I filed bankruptcy before?

If you had a bankruptcy case dismissed within the past year, the automatic stay in your new case lasts only 30 days unless the court extends it. Two or more dismissals in the past year means no stay at all unless the court orders it under section 362(c)(3) and (c)(4).

How long does the automatic stay last?

In Chapter 7, the stay lasts until your case is closed, dismissed, or discharge is granted (typically 3-4 months). In Chapter 13, it lasts for the duration of your 3-5 year plan. Creditors can file a motion for relief from stay.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/bank-account.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Bank Account

Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Houston

Filing bankruptcy does not mean your bank account is seized. Understanding how bank accounts work in bankruptcy helps Houston residents protect their money.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Happens to Your Bank Account When You File

When you file bankruptcy, the money in your bank account on the filing date becomes property of the bankruptcy estate. However, most or all of it may be protected by exemptions:

Exempt funds: Social Security, disability benefits, veterans' benefits, and other government payments are generally fully exempt from the bankruptcy estate.

Wages: Texas provides strong wage protection - current wages in your bank account may be traceable and exempt.

Timing matters: Try to file when your account balance is at its lowest point in the month (typically right after paying bills) to minimize the amount subject to the trustee's review.

The trustee reviews your statements: You must provide bank statements for the 60 days before filing.

Bank Account Freezes and Levies

Before Bankruptcy

Creditors with judgments can levy (seize) your bank account

Payday lenders with ACH authorization can drain your account

The IRS can levy without a court judgment

Garnishment orders can intercept deposits

After Filing Bankruptcy

The automatic stay stops all levies and freezes

Existing levies must be released

No new garnishment orders can be issued

Payday lender ACH debits must stop

Practical Steps for Houston Filers

Open a new account if needed: If a creditor has your account information and is making unauthorized withdrawals, open a new account at a different bank.

Do NOT owe money to your bank: If you have a credit card or loan with the same bank where you have your checking account, the bank may exercise its right of setoff. Open an account at a different bank before filing.

Keep your balance low on filing day: Pay your normal bills before filing. The less cash in the account, the less the trustee can examine.

Document exempt funds: If your account contains Social Security, pension, or other exempt deposits, keep records showing the source of the funds.

Direct deposit: Post-filing income (earned after the filing date) is generally not part of the bankruptcy estate in Chapter 7.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will the trustee take money from my bank account?

Only if the balance exceeds what is protected by exemptions. Social Security, disability, veterans' benefits, and other exempt funds are protected. Keep records showing the source of deposits. Timing your filing when the balance is low minimizes exposure.

Can my bank freeze my account when I file bankruptcy?

Banks sometimes place temporary holds on accounts when they receive notice of the filing. This is more common if you owe the bank money (credit card, loan). Opening an account at a bank where you have no debts avoids this problem.

What is the right of setoff?

If you owe money to the same bank where your checking account is, the bank can take funds from your account to pay your debt to them. This is called setoff. Move your accounts to a different bank before filing.

Are my wages protected in my bank account in Texas?

Texas does not allow consumer wage garnishment. Only child support, taxes, and student loans can be garnished from wages - one of only four states with this protection. Wages deposited in your bank account may retain their exempt character, but proving which funds are wages versus other income requires documentation.

Can I open a new bank account after filing bankruptcy?

Yes. You can open a new bank account during and after bankruptcy. Some banks may check ChexSystems, but many banks and credit unions will open accounts for bankruptcy filers.

What happens to my bank account in Chapter 13?

In Chapter 13, you keep your bank account and continue using it normally. Your disposable income goes to creditors through the trustee, but you manage your day-to-day banking as usual.

Protect Your Finances

Exemptions Guide
Discharge Screener

## Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13? - Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/buy-car-chapter-13.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13?

Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13?

A free guide for Texas residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D. Tex..

What You Need to Know

You need trustee approval and a court motion to purchase a vehicle during Chapter 13. The trustee will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary and whether the payment fits your budget. Interest rates are typically higher. Used vehicles are more likely to be approved.

Filed in S.D. Tex.. Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston TX.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13?

You need trustee approval and a court motion to purchase a vehicle during Chapter 13. The trustee will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary and whether the payment fits your budget. Interest rates are typically higher. Used vehicles are more likely to be approved.

Does this apply in Texas?

Yes. This applies to S.D. Tex.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

515 Rusk Ave, Houston TX.

## Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/buying-a-house.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Buying a House

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Houston

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from buying a home. Houston residents can qualify for mortgages after specific waiting periods, and Texas has unique advantages for homebuyers rebuilding after bankruptcy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Mortgage Waiting Periods After Bankruptcy

After Chapter 7

FHA loan: 2 years from discharge

VA loan: 2 years from discharge

USDA loan: 3 years from discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie): 4 years from discharge

Jumbo/portfolio: Varies by lender, often 4-7 years

After Chapter 13

FHA loan: 1 year into plan (with court permission and trustee approval)

VA loan: 1 year into plan (with court and trustee approval)

USDA loan: 1 year into plan

Conventional: 2 years from discharge, or 4 years from dismissal

During plan: Court permission required for new debt

Rebuilding Credit for a Houston Mortgage

Start immediately after discharge. Get a secured credit card and use it for small purchases, paying the balance in full each month.

Target a 620+ credit score for FHA loans (580 minimum with 3.5% down). Conventional loans typically require 680+.

Establish 2-3 credit lines. Secured card, credit builder loan, and a small installment loan create a diverse credit mix.

Save for a down payment. FHA requires 3.5% minimum. Conventional loans require 3-5%. Larger down payments get better rates.

Texas has no state income tax. This means more take-home pay available for saving and qualifying for a mortgage.

Texas Homebuyer Considerations

Homestead protection: Once you purchase, Texas provides a unlimited (up to 10 acres urban, 100 acres rural) homestead exemption - protecting your equity in future financial difficulties.

Property taxes: Texas property taxes vary by county. In Houston, plan for these in your budget alongside your mortgage payment.

First-time homebuyer programs: Texas offers down payment assistance programs that may be available even with a prior bankruptcy, depending on the program requirements.

Mortgage pre-approval: Once your waiting period has passed, get pre-approved before house hunting. This shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long after bankruptcy can I buy a house in Houston?

With FHA, as soon as 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan (with court approval). Conventional loans require 4 years after Chapter 7 or 2 years after Chapter 13 discharge.

What credit score do I need for a mortgage after bankruptcy?

FHA loans require a minimum 580 score for 3.5% down (500-579 with 10% down). Conventional loans typically require 680+. Most post-bankruptcy filers can reach FHA thresholds within 1-2 years of discharge.

Can I buy a house during Chapter 13?

Yes, but you need court permission and trustee approval. Most courts, including the Southern District of Texas, require at least 1 year of on-time plan payments before approving new debt.

Will I get a higher interest rate after bankruptcy?

Initially, yes. Expect rates 1-3% higher than prime borrowers. As your credit score improves and time passes from the bankruptcy, rates improve. Refinancing after 2-3 years of on-time mortgage payments is common.

What down payment assistance is available in Houston?

Multiple programs exist for Houston and Texas residents, including FHA down payment assistance, Texas housing finance authority programs, and local homebuyer assistance. Eligibility varies but bankruptcy history is often not an automatic disqualifier.

How does the Texas homestead exemption protect me after purchase?

Texas provides a unlimited (up to 10 acres urban, 100 acres rural) homestead exemption. If you face financial difficulties after purchasing, this protects your home equity from most creditors.

Plan Your Path to Homeownership

Credit After Bankruptcy
Discharge Screener

## Car Leases and Bankruptcy - Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/car-lease.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Car Leases and Bankruptcy

Car Leases and Bankruptcy

A free guide for Texas residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D. Tex..

What You Need to Know

You have 60 days after filing to decide whether to assume (keep) or reject (surrender) your car lease. If you are current on payments, you can typically keep the lease by assuming it. If behind, rejection discharges the remaining obligation. Lease buyout may be an option if you want to own the vehicle.

Filed in S.D. Tex.. Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston TX.

Frequently Asked Questions

Car Leases and Bankruptcy

You have 60 days after filing to decide whether to assume (keep) or reject (surrender) your car lease. If you are current on payments, you can typically keep the lease by assuming it. If behind, rejection discharges the remaining obligation. Lease buyout may be an option if you want to own the vehicle.

Does this apply in Texas?

Yes. This applies to S.D. Tex.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

515 Rusk Ave, Houston TX.

## Chapter 13 Eligibility in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/chapter-13-eligibility.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Eligibility

Chapter 13 Eligibility in Houston

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property and repay debts over 3-5 years. Houston residents need regular income and debts within federal limits to file in the Southern District of Texas.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Basic Eligibility Requirements

To file Chapter 13 bankruptcy in the Southern District of Texas, you must meet these requirements:

Regular income: You need sufficient regular income to fund a repayment plan. This includes wages, self-employment income, Social Security, pension, or other regular payments.

Debt limits: Your total debts (secured and unsecured combined) must be under $2,750,000 (current limit as of 2024).

Tax returns: You must have filed all required tax returns for the 4 years before filing.

Credit counseling: Complete an approved credit counseling course within 180 days before filing.

No prior dismissal: If a prior case was dismissed within 180 days for failure to appear or comply with court orders, you cannot file immediately.

How Income Determines Your Plan Length

Below Texas Median

If your income is below the Texas median ($60,840 for one person), your repayment plan can be as short as 3 years (36 months). You have the option to propose a longer plan up to 5 years.

Above Texas Median

If your income exceeds the median, your plan must be 5 years (60 months). Your disposable income after allowed expenses goes to creditors through the plan. The means test determines your disposable income.

Why Houston Residents Choose Chapter 13

Save your home: Cure mortgage arrears over 3-5 years while keeping your home. Non-judicial foreclosure in as few as 21 days. Sales occur the first Tuesday of each month.

Keep your car: Pay the vehicle's value (cramdown) if the loan is more than 910 days old. Texas exempts one vehicle per licensed household member if needed for work.

Strip junior liens: If your home is worth less than the first mortgage, second mortgages may be removed entirely.

Protect co-debtors: The Chapter 13 co-debtor stay (section 1301) protects co-signers from collection on consumer debts.

Income too high for Chapter 7: If you do not pass the means test, Chapter 13 is your path to debt relief.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the debt limits for Chapter 13 in Houston?

Your total debts - secured and unsecured combined - must be under $2,750,000 (current limit). There is no minimum debt requirement.

Do I need a job to file Chapter 13 in Texas?

You need regular income, but it does not have to come from employment. Social Security, pension, disability, rental income, or support from a spouse or household member can all qualify.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan?

If your income is below the Texas median ($60,840 for one person), the plan can be 3 years. Above median, it must be 5 years. No plan can exceed 5 years.

Can I file Chapter 13 after a Chapter 7 discharge?

Yes. You can file Chapter 13 after a Chapter 7 discharge, but you must wait 4 years from the Chapter 7 filing date to receive a Chapter 13 discharge. You can still use Chapter 13 for its repayment benefits even without a discharge (called a Chapter 20). Check timing with the 1328(f) screener.

What happens if I cannot make plan payments?

You can modify the plan, convert to Chapter 7, or request a hardship discharge if you have paid most of your plan and the failure was due to circumstances beyond your control. The Southern District of Texas requires prompt notification.

What is the dismissal rate for Chapter 13 in Texas?

Chapter 13 has a higher dismissal rate than Chapter 7 nationally. Many cases are dismissed for failure to make plan payments or file required documents. Staying current on payments is critical.

Check Your Chapter 13 Options

Chapter 7 vs 13
Discharge Screener

## Chapter 13 Payments in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/chapter-13-payments.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Payments

Chapter 13 Payments in Houston

Chapter 13 bankruptcy requires monthly payments over 3-5 years. Understanding how payments are calculated, distributed, and managed is essential for Houston filers in the Southern District of Texas.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Chapter 13 Payments Are Calculated

Your monthly payment is based on several factors:

Disposable income: Income minus allowed living expenses as determined by the means test

Secured debt obligations: Mortgage arrears, car payments, and other secured debts that must be paid through the plan

Priority debts: Tax debts, domestic support obligations, and other priority claims must be paid in full

Best-interest-of-creditors test: Unsecured creditors must receive at least as much as they would in a Chapter 7 liquidation

Trustee fee: The Chapter 13 trustee takes a percentage (typically 4-10%) of each payment for administrative costs

What Gets Paid Through the Plan

Paid in Full (100%)

Mortgage arrears (to cure default)

Priority tax debts

Domestic support arrears

Administrative expenses (trustee fees, attorney fees)

Secured debt payments (car loans, etc.)

Paid Partially (Often Pennies on Dollar)

Credit card balances

Medical bills

Personal loans

Payday loans

Remaining unsecured debt (whatever your disposable income allows)

Plan Length and Texas Income

Below median income ($60,840 for 1 person in Texas): Minimum plan is 36 months (3 years), maximum is 60 months (5 years)

Above median income: Plan must be 60 months (5 years)

Texas has no state income tax: This means more take-home pay is measured by the means test, potentially resulting in higher plan payments but also leaving more room for living expenses.

First payment due within 30 days of filing. Payments are typically made via wage deduction or direct payment to the trustee.

What Happens If You Miss a Payment

Missing Chapter 13 payments is the number one reason cases are dismissed. If you fall behind:

The trustee may file a motion to dismiss your case, which lifts the automatic stay and exposes you to creditor collection

You can request a plan modification if your income has decreased or expenses have increased

You can convert to Chapter 7 if you can no longer afford any plan payment

Hardship discharge: In rare cases, if you have paid most of the plan and the failure was beyond your control, the court may grant a hardship discharge

Communication is key: Contact the trustee and your attorney immediately if you are having trouble making payments

Frequently Asked Questions

How much are Chapter 13 payments in Houston?

Payments vary based on your income, expenses, and debts. There is no fixed amount. The means test determines your disposable income. Texas median income for one person is $60,840 - if below this, your plan can be 3 years; above, it must be 5 years.

When is my first Chapter 13 payment due?

Your first payment must be made within 30 days of filing. Payments are typically monthly via wage deduction or direct payment to the Chapter 13 trustee.

Can my Chapter 13 payments change?

Yes. You can file a plan modification if your financial situation changes - income increase or decrease, new expenses, or changes in debt. The court must approve modifications.

What happens if I miss a Chapter 13 payment?

The trustee may move to dismiss your case. Contact your attorney immediately. You may be able to modify the plan, catch up on payments, or convert to Chapter 7. The Southern District of Texas expects prompt communication.

Do I make my mortgage payment through the plan or directly?

Practices vary. Some plans include the regular mortgage payment; others only include arrears while you pay the mortgage directly. The trustee in the Southern District of Texas will specify how payments are structured.

What percentage of my debt will I actually pay?

Priority debts and secured debts are paid in full. Unsecured creditors receive whatever your disposable income allows - often 10-30% of the total, sometimes less. The remainder is discharged at plan completion.

Understand Your Options

Chapter 13 Plan Guide
Discharge Screener

Houston Exemptions

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Houston - Payment Plans, Dismissal Rates, Timeline
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Houston

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. But in the S.D. Tex., the dismissal rate is 65.1% - meaning many filers never complete their plan.

65.1% of S.D. Tex. Chapter 13 cases are dismissed

65.1%

S.D. Tex. Dismissal Rate

34.9%

Discharge Rate

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors according to the plan.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

S.D. Tex. Dismissal Rate

The data tells a concerning story. A dismissed Chapter 13 means the debtor spent months or years making payments but received no discharge - no debt relief.

Southern District of Texas (S.D. Tex.)

65.1% dismissed

Out of 91,806 Chapter 13 cases filed between 2008 and 2024, only 34.9% achieved a successful discharge. The prior filer rate is 30.6% - nearly 1 in 4 filers had a prior case.

Why it matters: A dismissed Chapter 13 means the automatic stay ends, creditors resume collection, and the debtor receives no discharge. Months or years of payments produced no lasting relief. Strong legal representation is critical to completing a Chapter 13 plan. View full statistics.

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income - the difference between your income and your reasonable living expenses. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills, etc. - often pennies on the dollar

Plan Duration: 3 or 5 Years?

If your income is below the state median, you may be able to complete a 3-year plan. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The court will not approve a plan longer than 5 years.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. This eliminates remaining eligible unsecured debts. You keep your home, car, and other property.

If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent you from receiving another discharge. Check your eligibility before filing.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 - Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors. The trustee reviews your plan and finances.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate in Houston?

The Southern District of Texas has a 65.1% Chapter 13 dismissal rate based on 2008-2024 data. A dismissed case means the debtor got no debt relief despite months or years of payments.

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Chapter 13 plans last 3 to 5 years. If your income is below the state median, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above the median, your plan must be 5 years.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. One of the main benefits of Chapter 13 is the ability to keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments. The automatic stay stops foreclosure proceedings immediately upon filing.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

If your Chapter 13 case is dismissed, the automatic stay ends and creditors can resume collection. You may be able to file again, but there may be waiting periods.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 7 Eligibility in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/chapter-7-eligibility.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 7 Eligibility

Chapter 7 Eligibility in Houston

Chapter 7 provides the fastest path to debt relief, but you must qualify. Houston residents must pass the means test and meet other requirements to file in the Southern District of Texas.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Means Test in Texas

The means test compares your household income to the Texas median. If your income is below the median, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7. The current Texas median income for a single person is $60,840.

Below median: You qualify for Chapter 7 automatically

Above median: You must complete the full means test (Form 122A-2), which subtracts allowed expenses from your income. If disposable income is low enough, you still qualify.

Texas has no state income tax, which affects the means test calculation - Texas residents keep more take-home pay but that higher take-home pay is what the means test measures

Take the means test at our Houston means test page or visit bankruptcymeanstest.org for a detailed explanation.

Other Eligibility Requirements

You Must

Complete credit counseling within 180 days before filing

Not have received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years

Not have received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years

Not have had a case dismissed for abuse within 180 days

File tax returns for the past 4 years

Disqualifying Factors

Income above the means test threshold with no allowable deductions

Prior discharge within the time bar period

Prior case dismissed within 180 days for willful failure to comply

Attempting to defraud creditors (hiding assets, recent luxury spending)

Failure to complete required credit counseling

Texas Exemptions That Protect Your Property

Filing Chapter 7 does not mean you lose everything. Texas exemptions protect:

Homestead: unlimited (up to 10 acres urban, 100 acres rural)

Vehicle: one vehicle per licensed household member if needed for work

Retirement accounts: IRAs, 401(k)s, and pensions are fully exempt under federal and Texas law

Personal property: Clothing, household furnishings, and tools of the trade up to Texas limits

See our full Houston exemptions guide for complete details.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Houston?

The means test uses Texas median income: $60,840 for a single person. If below this, you qualify automatically. If above, you may still qualify after subtracting allowed expenses.

How often can I file Chapter 7 in Texas?

You must wait 8 years from a prior Chapter 7 discharge, or 6 years from a prior Chapter 13 discharge. Check your eligibility with the 1328(f) screener.

Do I have to pass the means test?

Yes, unless you are a disabled veteran or your debts are primarily business debts. All consumer debtors must complete the means test form even if they qualify automatically.

Can I file Chapter 7 if I own a home?

Yes. Texas's unlimited (up to 10 acres urban, 100 acres rural) homestead exemption protects your home equity. If your equity exceeds the exemption, Chapter 13 may be a better option.

What happens to my car in Chapter 7 in Texas?

Texas exempts one vehicle per licensed household member if needed for work. If your equity is within this limit, you keep your vehicle. If you owe more than the car is worth, you can reaffirm or surrender.

How long does Chapter 7 take?

Most Chapter 7 cases in the Southern District of Texas take 3-4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting occurs about 30-40 days after filing.

Check Your Eligibility

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Houston - Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Houston

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Houston residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors - but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In Houston, the vast majority of Chapter 7 filers keep all their property because it falls within Texas exemption limits.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the state median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility. If you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan, you may be required to file Chapter 13 instead

For a single filer in Texas, the current median is approximately $56,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Texas Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep. Texas requires you to use state exemptions - you cannot choose the federal exemption system.

PropertyTexas ExemptionStatute

HomesteadUnlimited (10 acres urban / 100 acres rural)Tex. Prop. Code 41.001

VehicleFully exempt (1 per licensed household member)Tex. Prop. Code 42.002

Personal property (aggregate)$60,000 married / $30,000 singleTex. Prop. Code 42.001

ClothingIncluded in personal propertyTex. Prop. Code 42.002

Tools of tradeUnlimitedTex. Prop. Code 42.002

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)UnlimitedTex. Prop. Code 42.0021

Social Security benefitsUnlimited42 U.S.C. 407

Wages (current)Unlimited (non-garnishable in Texas)Tex. Prop. Code 42.001

Life insurance (cash value)UnlimitedTex. Ins. Code 1108.051

Health aidsUnlimitedTex. Prop. Code 42.002

Full exemption details.

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation - Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing - Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately - creditors must stop all collection activity, garnishments, lawsuits, and foreclosure proceedings.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting - Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education - Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge - The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts. Case closed shortly after.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, alimony, or debts incurred through fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7?

The means test compares your household income to the Texas median. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify. If above, a more detailed calculation determines whether you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan instead.

How long does Chapter 7 take?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting of creditors occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in Texas?

Texas exemptions protect: homestead equity of Unlimited (10 acres urban), vehicle equity of $0 - fully exempt if needed for work, personal property of $60,000 (married) / $30,000 (single), and retirement accounts. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/checklist.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Checklist

Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for Houston

Use this checklist to prepare for your bankruptcy filing in the S.D. Tex.. Gathering these documents before meeting with an attorney saves time and money.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Documents You Will Need

Income Documentation

Pay stubs for the last 6 months (all jobs)

Federal tax returns for the last 2 years

No state income tax returns required in Texas

Social Security, pension, or retirement income statements

Self-employment profit and loss statements (if applicable)

Unemployment benefit records (if applicable)

Asset Documentation

Bank statements for the last 3-6 months (all accounts)

Vehicle titles and registration

Mortgage statement showing balance and monthly payment

Property tax assessment or recent appraisal

Retirement account statements (401k, IRA, pension)

Life insurance policies (with cash value)

Debt Documentation

Credit reports from all three bureaus (free at annualcreditreport.com)

Collection letters and lawsuit papers

Medical bills, student loan, car loan, and credit card statements

Garnishment orders

Personal Documentation

Government-issued photo ID

Social Security card

Proof of current address

Marriage certificate or divorce decree (if applicable)

Child support or alimony orders (if applicable)

Required Courses

Pre-Filing Credit Counseling

Within 180 days before filing

60-90 minutes, online or phone

~$25, DOJ-approved provider

Certificate filed with petition

Post-Filing Debtor Education

Before discharge

~2 hours, online or phone

~$25, DOJ-approved provider

Certificate filed with court

Filing Costs in the S.D. Tex.

ExpenseChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Credit counseling~$25~$25

Debtor education~$25~$25

Attorney fees (Houston range)$1,000-$2,500$3,000-$5,000

Total estimate$1,388-$2,888$3,363-$5,363

Filing fees can be paid in installments. Fee waivers available for filers below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Things to Avoid Before Filing

Do not transfer property to friends, family, or trusts

Do not pay back relatives - payments within 1 year to insiders can be recovered

Do not run up credit card debt - charges over $800 on luxury goods within 90 days are presumed non-dischargeable

Do not cash out retirement accounts - they are fully exempt in bankruptcy

Do not hide assets - this is federal bankruptcy fraud

Free Legal Help in Houston

Lone Star Legal Aid (1-800-733-8394), Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732)

The S.D. Tex. bankruptcy clerk's office at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002 can also provide pro se resources.

Frequently Asked Questions

What documents do I need to file bankruptcy in Houston?

You need: 6 months of pay stubs, 2 years of tax returns, 3-6 months of bank statements, vehicle titles, mortgage statements, a complete debt list, and credit reports from all three bureaus.

Do I need to take a class before filing?

Yes. Credit counseling from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. 60-90 minutes, ~$25, available online.

Do I need to take a class after filing?

Yes. A debtor education course (different from pre-filing counseling) before receiving discharge. ~2 hours, ~$25.

How much does it cost to file in Houston?

Chapter 7: $338 filing fee + ~$1,000-2,500 attorney. Chapter 13: $313 filing fee + ~$3,000-5,000 attorney. Credit counseling: ~$25 each course.

Can I file without an attorney?

Technically yes (pro se), but not recommended. Lone Star Legal Aid (1-800-733-8394), Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732) may help if you cannot afford an attorney.

What should I NOT do before filing?

Do not: transfer property, pay back relatives, run up credit card debt, cash out retirement, or hide assets.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Bankruptcy in Baytown, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/baytown.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Baytown, TX

Bankruptcy in Baytown, TX

Baytown is an industrial city east of Houston at the convergence of the San Jacinto River and Galveston Bay. Home to major refinery operations, Baytown's economy is closely tied to the energy sector. Economic downturns in oil and gas can create sudden financial hardship for families.

Key Information for Baytown Residents

County

Harris County (parts in Chambers County)

Population: ~83,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Baytown are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

77520775217752277523

Texas Exemptions for Baytown Residents

Baytown residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. Texas's strong exemption laws protect your homestead (unlimited value, up to 10 urban acres), vehicles (one per licensed household member), personal property (up to $50,000 per individual), and retirement accounts (fully exempt). For refinery workers facing cyclical layoffs, Chapter 13 can provide structured catch-up on debts while Chapter 7 offers a complete fresh start.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Baytown

The Houston bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is approximately 25 miles west via I-10. After filing, the automatic stay takes effect immediately. Expect your 341 meeting about 30 days after filing. Chapter 7 cases typically wrap up in 3-4 months.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Baytown Residents

Baytown residents can access Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077) for free legal assistance. The Harris County Law Library is available for legal research. Court-approved credit counseling is available online.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Baytown, TX
I was laid off from the refinery - should I file bankruptcy immediately?

Timing matters. If you expect to be re-employed soon, waiting may not be necessary. However, if debts are mounting, filing sooner stops collection activity through the automatic stay. A recent job loss can help you qualify for Chapter 7 since the means test looks at your six-month average income.

Can I keep my truck if I file bankruptcy in Baytown?

Texas allows you to exempt at least one motor vehicle per licensed household member. If you need your truck for work, it is likely protected. If you owe money on the truck, Chapter 13 lets you catch up on payments, and Chapter 7 allows you to reaffirm the debt.

Will bankruptcy discharge medical debt from a workplace injury?

Yes. Medical debt is unsecured debt that is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. If you have a pending workers compensation claim, that is a separate process and is generally not affected by bankruptcy.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

Other Houston Area Cities

Sugar LandPearlandKatyThe WoodlandsPasadenaLeague CityMissouri CityCypressSpringHumbleConroeGalvestonBeaumontFriendswoodAll Cities →

## Bankruptcy in Beaumont, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/beaumont.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Beaumont, TX

Bankruptcy in Beaumont, TX

Beaumont is the largest city in Jefferson County and the seat of the Beaumont-Port Arthur metro area. The local economy centers on petrochemical refining, healthcare, and Lamar University. Hurricanes and energy sector downturns have created financial hardship for many families.

Key Information for Beaumont Residents

County

Jefferson County

Population: ~115,000

Federal District

Eastern District of Texas, Beaumont Division

All bankruptcy cases from Beaumont are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

300 Willow St, Beaumont, TX 77701

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

7770177702777037770577706777077770877713

Texas Exemptions for Beaumont Residents

Beaumont residents file in the Eastern District of Texas, Beaumont Division - not the Southern District like most Houston-area suburbs. The courthouse is at 300 Willow St, Beaumont, TX 77701. Texas exemptions apply statewide, so Beaumont residents get the same unlimited homestead exemption, vehicle protections, and personal property exemptions. The Eastern District may have different local rules and trustee practices.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Beaumont

Unlike other Houston-area suburbs, Beaumont has its own federal courthouse at 300 Willow St - you do not have to commute to Houston. Your 341 meeting will typically be held locally. The Eastern District has its own panel of bankruptcy trustees.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Eastern District of Texas, Beaumont Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Beaumont Residents

Beaumont residents can access Lone Star Legal Aid's Beaumont office directly. The Jefferson County Bar Association offers referrals. Southeast Texas Legal Aid provides services to low-income residents.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Beaumont, TX
Do Beaumont residents file in the same court as Houston?

No. Beaumont is in the Eastern District of Texas, not the Southern District. Your case is filed at 300 Willow St, Beaumont, TX 77701. You do not have to commute to Houston.

Are the bankruptcy rules different in Beaumont than Houston?

Texas exemptions apply statewide, so property protections are the same. However, the Eastern District has its own local rules, trustee panel, and court procedures. Attorney fee guidelines and plan requirements can vary between districts.

Can I discharge hurricane debt in Beaumont?

Yes. Unsecured debts incurred for hurricane repairs - credit cards, personal loans, contractor bills - are fully dischargeable. SBA disaster loans are also dischargeable. Insurance claims and FEMA assistance are separate from bankruptcy.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Conroe, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/conroe.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Conroe, TX

Bankruptcy in Conroe, TX

Conroe is the county seat of Montgomery County, about 40 miles north of downtown Houston. One of the fastest-growing cities in America, Conroe attracts residents seeking affordable housing. However, rapid growth does not prevent financial hardship, and many residents benefit from bankruptcy protections.

Key Information for Conroe Residents

County

Montgomery County

Population: ~100,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Conroe are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

773017730277303773047730577306

Texas Exemptions for Conroe Residents

Conroe residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. Federal bankruptcy cases are handled in Houston. Texas exemptions fully protect your homestead (unlimited value, 10 urban acres), vehicles, and personal property. Conroe's affordable housing means most homeowners have equity well within protected limits.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Conroe

The Houston bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is approximately 40 miles south via I-45. This is one of the longer commutes in the Houston metro. However, many 341 meetings are now conducted by telephone, reducing the need for travel.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Conroe Residents

Montgomery County residents can access Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077). The Montgomery County Bar Association provides referrals. Online credit counseling courses satisfy the pre-filing requirement.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Conroe, TX
Is the commute to the Houston courthouse a problem?

The 40-mile drive can be inconvenient, but many routine matters, including 341 meetings, are conducted by telephone or video. For contested hearings, you may need to appear at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Can I file bankruptcy if I just moved to Conroe?

You file where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved from another state, your exemptions may be determined by your previous state for up to two years.

Will bankruptcy affect my Lake Conroe property?

If Lake Conroe property is your primary residence, it is protected by the Texas homestead exemption. If it is a second home or investment property, it could be sold by the Chapter 7 trustee. Chapter 13 may allow you to keep non-exempt property.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

Other Houston Area Cities

Sugar LandPearlandKatyThe WoodlandsPasadenaBaytownLeague CityMissouri CityCypressSpringHumbleGalvestonBeaumontFriendswoodAll Cities →

## Bankruptcy in Cypress, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/cypress.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Cypress, TX

Bankruptcy in Cypress, TX

Cypress is an unincorporated community in northwest Harris County that has experienced explosive growth. With master-planned communities, excellent schools, and a suburban feel, Cypress has attracted families and professionals. However, high mortgage payments, medical bills, and consumer debt can create financial strain.

Key Information for Cypress Residents

County

Harris County

Population: ~190,000 (unincorporated)

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Cypress are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

7742977433

Texas Exemptions for Cypress Residents

Cypress is unincorporated but falls within Harris County and the Southern District of Texas. Texas's unlimited homestead exemption is critical for Cypress residents with significant home values. Being unincorporated does not affect your filing. Property taxes from multiple taxing entities (Harris County, Cy-Fair ISD, MUDs) can add up and are not dischargeable, but Chapter 13 can help you catch up.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Cypress

The Houston bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is approximately 25-30 miles southeast via US-290. After filing, the automatic stay halts all collection efforts. Your 341 meeting is typically scheduled 30 days after filing. Many meetings are by phone. Chapter 7 cases conclude in 3-4 months; Chapter 13 requires 3-5 years.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Cypress Residents

Cypress residents have access to Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077), Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732), and the Harris County Law Library. Court-approved credit counseling and debtor education courses are available online.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Cypress, TX
I live in an unincorporated area - does that matter for bankruptcy?

No. Whether you live in an incorporated city or unincorporated Harris County, you file in the same court (Southern District of Texas, Houston Division) and the same Texas exemptions apply.

Can I protect a large home in Cypress?

Yes. Texas's homestead exemption is unlimited in value for urban property on up to 10 acres. Even if your home is worth $500,000 or more, it is fully protected as long as it is your primary residence. This is one of the strongest homestead protections in the country.

What happens to my MUD fees in bankruptcy?

MUD taxes and assessments that are past due can be addressed in Chapter 13. Future MUD taxes continue as a normal obligation. These are essentially property taxes and are not dischargeable in bankruptcy.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

Other Houston Area Cities

Sugar LandPearlandKatyThe WoodlandsPasadenaBaytownLeague CityMissouri CitySpringHumbleConroeGalvestonBeaumontFriendswoodAll Cities →

## Bankruptcy in Friendswood, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/friendswood.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Friendswood, TX

Bankruptcy in Friendswood, TX

Friendswood is a suburban city straddling Galveston and Harris Counties between Houston and the Gulf Coast. Known for excellent schools and family orientation, Friendswood has a higher median income than much of the Houston metro. Nevertheless, medical crises, job loss, or natural disasters can overwhelm even well-established households.

Key Information for Friendswood Residents

County

Galveston County (parts in Harris County)

Population: ~41,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Friendswood are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

7754677549

Texas Exemptions for Friendswood Residents

Friendswood spans two counties but all residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. Texas's unlimited homestead exemption is particularly valuable in Friendswood where home values are above average. Personal property exemptions protect up to $50,000 per individual ($100,000 for families). Friendswood was significantly impacted by Hurricane Harvey.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Friendswood

The Houston bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is approximately 25 miles northwest. After filing, the automatic stay immediately stops all creditor activity. Your 341 meeting is scheduled about 30 days after filing and may be by phone. Chapter 7 typically concludes in 3-4 months.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Friendswood Residents

Friendswood residents can access Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077), the Galveston County Bar Association, and the Harris County Law Library. Financial counseling through nonprofit agencies is available. Court-approved credit counseling is online.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Friendswood, TX
Is Friendswood in the Houston or Galveston Division?

Friendswood cases are typically filed in the Houston Division (515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002). Some Galveston County cases may be assigned to the Galveston Division.

Can I protect my home equity in Friendswood?

Yes. Texas's homestead exemption is unlimited in value for your primary residence on up to 10 urban acres. Friendswood homes are fully protected regardless of how much equity you have.

What is the means test income limit?

The means test uses Texas statewide median income figures, updated periodically. For a household of four, the median is approximately $100,000 (check the U.S. Trustee's website for current figures). If below the median, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Galveston, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/galveston.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Galveston, TX

Bankruptcy in Galveston, TX

Galveston is an island city and county seat on the Gulf Coast, about 50 miles southeast of Houston. Tourism, healthcare (UTMB), and the port drive the local economy. Hurricane exposure, seasonal employment, and medical debt are common financial stressors.

Key Information for Galveston Residents

County

Galveston County

Population: ~53,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Galveston are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

77550775517755377554

Texas Exemptions for Galveston Residents

Galveston County cases may be filed in the Houston or Galveston Division of the Southern District of Texas. The Galveston federal courthouse at 601 25th St handles some matters. Texas exemptions apply, including the unlimited homestead exemption. For island property, the 10-acre urban limit applies. Galveston's hurricane vulnerability means many residents carry repair debt that bankruptcy can address.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Galveston

Cases may be heard at Galveston (601 25th St) or Houston (515 Rusk Ave, ~50 miles northwest). After filing, the automatic stay protects you from all collection activity. Seasonal workers should time filing carefully, as the means test looks at six months of income.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Galveston Residents

Galveston County residents can access Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077) and the Galveston County Bar Association. UTMB may have financial assistance for medical debt. Online credit counseling is available 24/7.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Galveston, TX
Can I file at the Galveston courthouse or do I have to go to Houston?

Some Galveston County cases are heard at the Galveston Division courthouse at 601 25th St. Others are assigned to Houston at 515 Rusk Ave. The assignment depends on your case and scheduling.

Is my beach house protected by the homestead exemption?

Only if it is your primary residence. Texas's homestead exemption applies to the property you actually live in, not vacation homes or rental properties. If your Galveston property is your primary home, it is fully protected.

Can bankruptcy help with hurricane-related debt?

Yes. Credit card debt, personal loans, contractor bills, and SBA disaster loans incurred for hurricane repairs are dischargeable. Insurance claims and FEMA assistance are separate processes.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Humble, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/humble.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Humble, TX

Bankruptcy in Humble, TX

Humble is a city in northeast Harris County, at the heart of a larger area that includes Kingwood, Atascocita, and Summerwood. The Greater Humble area serves as a bedroom community for Houston workers. Residents can face financial difficulties from job loss, medical emergencies, or accumulated debt.

Key Information for Humble Residents

County

Harris County

Population: ~200,000+ (Greater Humble area)

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Humble are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

77338773397734677396

Texas Exemptions for Humble Residents

Humble and surrounding communities file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. Texas exemptions are particularly favorable for homeowners, with unlimited homestead protection. The Greater Humble area was heavily impacted by Hurricane Harvey (2017). Residents who took on debt for repairs may find bankruptcy relief helpful. Retirement accounts are fully protected.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Humble

The Houston bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is approximately 20-25 miles southwest via US-59. The automatic stay takes effect the moment your petition is filed. Your 341 meeting will be scheduled about 30 days later. In a straightforward Chapter 7 case, expect discharge about 60 days after the 341 meeting.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Humble Residents

Humble area residents can access Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077), Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732), and the Harris County Law Library.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Humble, TX
Can I file bankruptcy if I still have Harvey-related debt?

Yes. Personal loans, credit card debt, and other unsecured obligations incurred for storm repairs are dischargeable. FEMA assistance received is not counted as income for the means test.

How do I know if I qualify for Chapter 7?

You must pass the means test, which compares your household income to the Texas median for your family size. If below the median, you automatically qualify. If above, a detailed expense analysis determines eligibility.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Certain debts survive bankruptcy: child support and alimony, most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), recent tax debts (generally within 3 years), debts from fraud or DUI injuries, and criminal fines. Most consumer debts are fully dischargeable.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

Find bankruptcy information specific to your Houston area city or suburb. Each page covers courthouse locations, Texas exemptions, local legal resources, zip codes, and what to expect when filing from your community.

Frequently Asked Questions
Which bankruptcy court do Houston area suburbs file in?

Most Houston area suburbs file in the same federal district court. Click on your city above for specific courthouse information, driving distances, and local resources.

Are Texas exemptions the same across all Houston suburbs?

Texas exemptions apply statewide, so all Houston area residents have the same property protections. However, the county you live in determines which federal district handles your case. View Texas exemptions.

How do I find a bankruptcy attorney near me in the Houston area?

Each city page above includes local legal aid resources, bar association referral services, and law school clinics. Many attorneys offer free initial consultations. You can also check our legal aid directory.
