## Bankruptcy in Katy, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/katy.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Katy, TX

Bankruptcy in Katy, TX

Katy is a rapidly growing community west of Houston that spans Harris, Fort Bend, and Waller Counties. The Greater Katy area is home to over 350,000 residents and is one of the most sought-after suburban areas in Texas. Financial difficulties can affect anyone, and Katy residents have full access to federal bankruptcy protections.

Key Information for Katy Residents

County

Harris County (parts in Fort Bend and Waller Counties)

Population: ~350,000+ (Greater Katy)

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Katy are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

774497745077491774927749377494

Texas Exemptions for Katy Residents

The Katy area is known for master-planned communities with significant home values. Texas's unlimited homestead exemption is particularly valuable here - your primary residence is fully protected regardless of value (up to 10 urban acres). Katy residents also benefit from Texas's generous personal property exemptions, including protection for two firearms, home furnishings, and up to $50,000 in personal property per individual ($100,000 for families). Retirement accounts are fully exempt.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Katy

Katy residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave in downtown Houston is approximately 30 miles east via I-10. Plan for the commute to any in-person hearings. Many 341 meetings are conducted by phone. After filing, the automatic stay immediately halts all collection calls, lawsuits, foreclosures, and repossessions.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Katy Residents

Legal aid options include Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077) and the Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732). The Fort Bend County Law Library and Harris County Law Library are both accessible to Katy residents.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Katy, TX
I live in a master-planned community in Katy - is my home protected?

Yes. Texas's unlimited homestead exemption protects your primary residence regardless of its value, as long as it sits on 10 acres or less of urban property. HOA dues and assessments are separate obligations that survive bankruptcy.

Will my HOA find out if I file bankruptcy in Katy?

Bankruptcy filings are public records. If you owe your HOA money, they will be listed as a creditor and notified. Current HOA dues that come due after filing are not discharged - you must continue paying them.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy from Katy?

The court filing fee is $338 for Chapter 7 and $313 for Chapter 13. Attorney fees in the Houston area typically range from $1,000-$2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,000-$5,000 for Chapter 13. You can request to pay the filing fee in installments.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

Other Houston Area Cities

Sugar LandPearlandThe WoodlandsPasadenaBaytownLeague CityMissouri CityCypressSpringHumbleConroeGalvestonBeaumontFriendswoodAll Cities →

## Bankruptcy in League City, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/league-city.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » League City, TX

Bankruptcy in League City, TX

League City is the largest city in Galveston County, located between Houston and Galveston along the I-45 corridor near NASA's Johnson Space Center. Despite its growth and prosperity, residents can face financial challenges from medical bills, credit card debt, or unexpected expenses.

Key Information for League City Residents

County

Galveston County (parts in Harris County)

Population: ~115,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from League City are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

7757377574

Texas Exemptions for League City Residents

League City straddles Galveston and Harris Counties. All residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division (some Galveston County cases may be assigned to the Galveston Division). Texas exemptions fully protect your homestead. Personal property exemptions cover up to $50,000 per individual ($100,000 for families). Higher incomes near the aerospace industry may require Chapter 13 rather than Chapter 7.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from League City

The Houston bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is about 30 miles northwest. Some Galveston County cases are heard at the Galveston federal courthouse (601 25th St). After filing, expect the 341 meeting within 30 days. Many are conducted telephonically. The automatic stay protects you from all collection activity from the moment you file.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for League City Residents

Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077) serves Galveston County. The Galveston County Bar Association offers referrals. University of Houston Law Center and South Texas College of Law clinics may accept Galveston County residents.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - League City, TX
Will my case be heard in Houston or Galveston?

League City cases are typically filed in the Houston Division (515 Rusk Ave, Houston). However, some Galveston County cases may be assigned to the Galveston Division (601 25th St, Galveston, TX 77550). Your filing attorney can advise which division applies.

Can I protect my boat if I file bankruptcy?

A boat may be protected under Texas's personal property exemptions ($50,000 individual / $100,000 family). If used for your livelihood, it may qualify for tools-of-trade protection. Recreational boats of high value may be at risk in Chapter 7.

How does hurricane damage affect my bankruptcy options?

If you have disaster-related debts or insurance shortfalls, bankruptcy can discharge those unsecured obligations. FEMA disaster assistance is not considered income for the means test. Your homestead exemption still applies to hurricane-damaged property.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

Sugar LandPearlandKatyThe WoodlandsPasadenaBaytownMissouri CityCypressSpringHumbleConroeGalvestonBeaumontFriendswoodAll Cities →

## Bankruptcy in Missouri City, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/missouri-city.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Missouri City, TX

Bankruptcy in Missouri City, TX

Missouri City is a diverse suburban community in Fort Bend County, located southwest of Houston between Sugar Land and Pearland. The city has a strong middle-class presence. Financial setbacks from medical emergencies, divorce, or job loss can create situations where bankruptcy provides a path forward.

Key Information for Missouri City Residents

County

Fort Bend County (parts in Harris County)

Population: ~75,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Missouri City are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

7745977489

Texas Exemptions for Missouri City Residents

Missouri City residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. Texas exemptions are among the most generous in the nation. Your homestead is fully protected regardless of value. Personal property exemptions cover household furnishings, clothing, tools of trade, and up to two firearms per family member. Fort Bend County's property tax rates are moderate, but falling behind can trigger a lien - Chapter 13 allows you to pay back property tax arrears over your plan.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Missouri City

The Houston bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is approximately 20 miles northeast. After filing, the automatic stay immediately protects you from creditor harassment, lawsuits, garnishments, and foreclosure. Your 341 meeting will be scheduled within 30 days. Bring valid photo ID and your Social Security card.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Missouri City Residents

Missouri City residents can access Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077), Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732), and the Fort Bend Lawyers Care program. The Fort Bend County Law Library is open to the public.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Missouri City, TX
Can I keep my car in bankruptcy if I live in Missouri City?

Texas exempts at least one motor vehicle per licensed household member. If your vehicle is paid off, it is protected. If you have a car loan, Chapter 13 lets you catch up on missed payments, and Chapter 7 lets you reaffirm the debt.

I am behind on property taxes - can bankruptcy help?

Chapter 13 allows you to pay back delinquent property taxes over your 3-5 year plan period while stopping a tax lien foreclosure through the automatic stay. Chapter 7 does not discharge property tax liens, but it can eliminate other debts.

Will bankruptcy affect my spouse?

Texas is a community property state. If only one spouse files, the automatic stay still protects community property. Joint debts may still be collected from the non-filing spouse. Many married couples file jointly to address all debts comprehensively.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Pasadena, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/pasadena.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Pasadena, TX

Bankruptcy in Pasadena, TX

Pasadena is the second-largest city in Harris County, located southeast of downtown Houston near the Houston Ship Channel. With a large working-class population and significant petrochemical industry presence, Pasadena residents face financial pressures from industry downturns, medical debt, and cost-of-living increases.

Key Information for Pasadena Residents

County

Harris County

Population: ~152,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Pasadena are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

7750177502775037750477505775067750777508

Texas Exemptions for Pasadena Residents

Pasadena is entirely within Harris County. Texas exemptions protect your home (unlimited value, up to 10 urban acres), one vehicle per licensed household member, and up to $50,000 in personal property ($100,000 for families). Texas also exempts 75% of wages from garnishment (90% for head of household), and retirement accounts are fully protected.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Pasadena

The bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave in downtown Houston is approximately 15 miles northwest of Pasadena. After filing, the automatic stay immediately stops wage garnishments, collection calls, lawsuits, and repossession attempts. Your 341 meeting will be scheduled about 30 days after filing.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Pasadena Residents

Pasadena residents have access to Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077), Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732), and the South Texas College of Law legal clinics. Many legal aid organizations provide bilingual services for Pasadena's significant Spanish-speaking population.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Pasadena, TX
Are there Spanish-speaking bankruptcy resources in Pasadena?

Yes. Lone Star Legal Aid and Houston Volunteer Lawyers both offer bilingual services. Many bankruptcy attorneys in the Houston area speak Spanish. Court-approved credit counseling courses are available in Spanish. The bankruptcy court also provides interpreters when needed.

Can I file bankruptcy if I was laid off from the petrochemical industry?

Yes. Unemployment or reduced income can actually make it easier to qualify for Chapter 7 because the means test looks at your average income over the six months before filing. A recent job loss may help you pass the means test.

Will bankruptcy stop a car repossession in Pasadena?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately stops repossession. In Chapter 13, you can catch up on missed car payments. In Chapter 7, you can reaffirm the debt to keep the vehicle. Texas allows you to exempt at least one vehicle per licensed household member.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Pearland, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/pearland.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Pearland, TX

Bankruptcy in Pearland, TX

Pearland is one of the fastest-growing cities in Texas, straddling the line between Brazoria and Harris Counties south of Houston. Its rapid growth has brought both opportunity and financial pressure. Residents facing overwhelming debt have the same bankruptcy protections available to all Texans.

Key Information for Pearland Residents

County

Brazoria County (parts in Harris County)

Population: ~125,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Pearland are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

775817758477588

Texas Exemptions for Pearland Residents

Pearland spans two counties, but all Pearland residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. Texas's unlimited homestead exemption protects your primary residence (up to 10 urban acres). The vehicle exemption allows one vehicle per licensed household member. Texas also protects up to $50,000 in personal property per individual ($100,000 for families), retirement accounts without limit, and 75% of wages (90% for head of household).

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Pearland

The Houston bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is about 20 miles north of Pearland via Highway 288. After filing, expect your 341 meeting approximately 30 days later. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Chapter 7 cases typically conclude in 3-4 months. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Pearland Residents

Pearland residents can contact Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077) for free legal assistance. The Houston Bar Association Lawyer Referral Service (713-237-9429) can connect you with bankruptcy attorneys who offer free initial consultations.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Pearland, TX
I live in the Brazoria County part of Pearland - where do I file?

All Pearland residents, whether in the Brazoria or Harris County portion, file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The courthouse is at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Will bankruptcy affect my ability to buy a new home?

A Chapter 7 discharge remains on your credit report for 10 years, and Chapter 13 for 7 years. However, many people qualify for FHA loans within 2 years of a Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan with court approval.

Can creditors garnish my wages in Pearland before I file?

Texas law generally prohibits wage garnishment for most consumer debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans). Exceptions include child support, student loans, and tax debts. Once you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay stops virtually all collection activity immediately.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Spring, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/spring.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Spring, TX

Bankruptcy in Spring, TX

Spring is an unincorporated community in northern Harris County along the I-45 corridor. The area includes both established neighborhoods and newer master-planned developments. Spring residents face the same financial pressures as others in the Houston metro - medical debt, credit cards, and natural disaster aftermath.

Key Information for Spring Residents

County

Harris County

Population: ~200,000+ (Greater Spring)

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Spring are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

773737737977383773867738877389

Texas Exemptions for Spring Residents

Spring falls within Harris County and the Southern District of Texas. Texas exemptions protect your homestead (unlimited value, up to 10 urban acres), vehicles (at least one per licensed driver), and personal property (up to $50,000 per individual). Spring's flood-prone location means some residents carry additional financial burdens from hurricane and flooding damage.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Spring

The Houston bankruptcy courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is approximately 25 miles south via I-45. After filing, the automatic stay provides immediate protection. Your 341 meeting is usually scheduled about 30 days after filing. Many meetings are conducted telephonically.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Spring Residents

Spring residents can contact Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077), Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732), or the Houston Bar Association Lawyer Referral Service (713-237-9429).

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Spring, TX
Can bankruptcy help with flood damage debt in Spring?

Yes. Debt from repairing flood damage, personal loans for repairs, or credit card debt from disaster expenses are unsecured debts that can be discharged in bankruptcy. FEMA grants are not considered income for the means test.

Will I lose my tax refund if I file bankruptcy?

A tax refund is considered an asset in bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, the trustee may claim all or part of your refund. Timing your filing strategically - such as filing after you receive and spend your refund on necessary expenses - is a common approach.

How does bankruptcy affect my credit score long term?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years, Chapter 13 for 7 years. However, many people see their scores begin to recover within 1-2 years of discharge as the debt-to-income ratio improves dramatically.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in Sugar Land, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/sugar-land.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » Sugar Land, TX

Bankruptcy in Sugar Land, TX

Sugar Land is one of the most affluent communities in the Houston metro area, located about 20 miles southwest of downtown Houston in Fort Bend County. Despite its prosperity, residents can face unexpected financial hardship from medical emergencies, job loss, or business failure. Filing bankruptcy in Sugar Land follows the same federal process as filing anywhere in the Southern District of Texas.

Key Information for Sugar Land Residents

County

Fort Bend County

Population: ~118,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from Sugar Land are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

7747877479774877749677498

Texas Exemptions for Sugar Land Residents

Sugar Land residents benefit from Texas's powerful exemptions, including an unlimited homestead exemption that protects your primary residence regardless of value (with acreage limits of 10 acres for urban property). Fort Bend County has seen steady growth, and many residents carry significant mortgage debt. If you are behind on your mortgage, Chapter 13 allows you to catch up on missed payments while keeping your home. The vehicle exemption allows one vehicle per licensed household member. Personal property exemptions cover up to $50,000 per individual ($100,000 for families).

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from Sugar Land

From Sugar Land, you will file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The courthouse is approximately 20 miles northeast in downtown Houston. Your 341 meeting of creditors will typically be scheduled about 30 days after filing. Many 341 meetings in the S.D. Tex. are now conducted by telephone or video, which can be convenient for Sugar Land residents.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for Sugar Land Residents

Fort Bend County residents can access free legal aid through Lone Star Legal Aid (713-652-0077) and the Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732). The Fort Bend County Law Library is open to the public. Pre-filing credit counseling and post-filing debtor education courses are available online from approved providers listed on the U.S. Trustee's website.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - Sugar Land, TX
Do I have to drive to downtown Houston for my bankruptcy hearing?

Your case will be filed in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division, with the courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002 - about 20 miles from Sugar Land. However, many 341 meetings are now conducted by phone or video conference, reducing the need for in-person appearances.

Can I keep my home in Sugar Land if I file bankruptcy?

Texas has an unlimited homestead exemption for your primary residence, subject to a 10-acre limit for urban properties. This means your Sugar Land home is fully protected in bankruptcy regardless of its value, as long as it is on 10 acres or less and is your primary residence.

What if I have a high income - can I still file Chapter 7?

You must pass the means test. Texas median income thresholds are applied statewide. If your income exceeds the median for your household size, you may still qualify for Chapter 7 if your disposable income after allowed expenses is low enough. Otherwise, Chapter 13 is available regardless of income level.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in The Woodlands, TX - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cities/the-woodlands.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCitiesFAQOBP

Home » Cities » The Woodlands, TX

Bankruptcy in The Woodlands, TX

The Woodlands is a master-planned community about 30 miles north of downtown Houston, primarily in Montgomery County. Known for its high quality of life and above-average incomes, The Woodlands is not immune to financial hardship. Medical debt, business failure, and divorce can overwhelm even high-income households.

Key Information for The Woodlands Residents

County

Montgomery County (parts in Harris County)

Population: ~120,000

Federal District

Southern District of Texas, Houston Division

All bankruptcy cases from The Woodlands are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Zip Codes Served

77380773817738277384773857738677389

Texas Exemptions for The Woodlands Residents

The Woodlands has some of the highest home values in the Houston metro area. Texas's unlimited homestead exemption is especially important here, fully protecting your primary residence regardless of value (up to 10 urban acres). For high-income residents who may not pass the Chapter 7 means test, Chapter 13 provides an alternative that allows you to restructure debts while keeping all your property. Texas also fully exempts retirement accounts, life insurance cash values, and college savings plans.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full Texas exemptions guide.

What to Expect Filing from The Woodlands

Residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The courthouse at 515 Rusk Ave is about 30 miles south via I-45. Some 341 meetings are conducted telephonically. If your case requires a court hearing, plan for the commute to downtown Houston.

The Filing Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25). Available online 24/7.

File your petition - Your petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors. Bring photo ID and your Social Security card. Many meetings are now by phone.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year repayment plan.

Local Resources for The Woodlands Residents

Lone Star Legal Aid serves Montgomery County residents (713-652-0077). The Montgomery County Bar Association offers a lawyer referral service. The U.S. Trustee's website lists approved credit counseling and debtor education providers.

Before You File

Gather your financial documents: pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, and a list of all debts

Complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved provider

Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney about your specific situation

Understand the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility using the 1328(f) screener

Frequently Asked Questions - The Woodlands, TX
Can high-income residents of The Woodlands file Chapter 7?

You must pass the means test. If your household income exceeds the Texas median for your family size, you may still qualify if your disposable income after allowed expenses is low enough. If not, Chapter 13 allows you to repay what you can afford over 3-5 years while protecting your assets.

Will filing bankruptcy affect my business?

Personal bankruptcy does not directly affect a separately incorporated business (LLC or corporation). However, if you personally guaranteed business debts, those guarantees can be addressed in your personal bankruptcy. Sole proprietorship assets may be at risk in Chapter 7.

How do I protect my retirement accounts in bankruptcy?

Retirement accounts including 401(k)s, IRAs, pensions, and other ERISA-qualified plans are fully exempt in Texas bankruptcy. Traditional and Roth IRAs are protected up to approximately $1.5 million. These protections apply regardless of which chapter you file.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.
Free Discharge Screener

Other Houston Area Cities

## Converting Between Bankruptcy Chapters in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/converting-chapters.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Converting Chapters

Converting Between Bankruptcy Chapters in Houston

Sometimes the chapter you filed under is not the right fit. Federal law allows you to convert between chapters, and Southern District of Texas filers should understand their rights and limitations.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Converting Chapter 13 to Chapter 7

Under 11 U.S.C. section 1307(a), you have an absolute right to convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, as long as your case was not originally a Chapter 7 that was converted to Chapter 13. This right cannot be denied by the court.

Common when you lose income and can no longer afford plan payments

You must still pass the means test for Chapter 7 - Texas median income for one person is $60,840

Any payments already made to creditors through your Chapter 13 plan are not returned to you

Your Texas exemptions apply - including the unlimited (up to 10 acres urban, 100 acres rural) homestead exemption

The automatic stay remains in effect during conversion

Converting Chapter 7 to Chapter 13

Under 11 U.S.C. section 706(a), you have a one-time right to convert from Chapter 7 to Chapter 13, provided you are eligible for Chapter 13 (regular income and debts within limits). Common reasons include:

You are about to lose your home or vehicle and need the repayment plan structure

The Chapter 7 trustee identified non-exempt assets you want to protect

You have debts that are non-dischargeable in Chapter 7 but can be managed in Chapter 13 (such as certain tax debts)

Your income increased and you no longer pass the means test

Chapter 13 debt limits: unsecured debts must be under $2,750,000 (combined secured and unsecured, effective 2024).

Houston-Specific Considerations

Homestead: Texas's unlimited (up to 10 acres urban, 100 acres rural) homestead exemption applies in both chapters. Converting to protect your home may be unnecessary if your equity is already exempt.

Vehicle: Texas exempts one vehicle per licensed household member if needed for work. If your vehicle equity exceeds this, Chapter 13 lets you keep it by paying the excess through the plan.

Foreclosure: Non-judicial foreclosure in as few as 21 days. Sales occur the first Tuesday of each month. If you are behind on your mortgage, converting to Chapter 13 lets you cure arrears over 3-5 years.

Filing location: Conversions are filed in the same court - the Southern District of Texas at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 in Houston?

Yes. You have an absolute right to convert under section 1307(a), as long as your case did not start as Chapter 7. File a motion in the Southern District of Texas and the conversion is automatic.

Can I convert from Chapter 7 to Chapter 13 in Houston?

Yes. You have a one-time right under section 706(a) if you are eligible for Chapter 13. This is common when you need to save a home or vehicle.

Do I need court permission to convert?

For Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, no - it is an absolute right. For Chapter 7 to Chapter 13, the court must confirm you are eligible but the conversion itself is a right.

What happens to my automatic stay when I convert?

The automatic stay continues throughout the conversion. There is no gap in protection.

Can I convert more than once?

You can convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 only once per case. You can convert from Chapter 7 to Chapter 13 only once per case. Serial conversions are not permitted.

How long does conversion take?

In the Southern District of Texas, conversion is typically processed within a few days of filing the motion. The case then proceeds under the new chapter with new deadlines.

Compare Your Chapter Options

Chapter 7 vs 13
Means Test Guide

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Houston? - Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/cost.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Houston?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

$1,000-$5,000

Typical Attorney Fees

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,000-$2,500Typical Houston range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free from annualcreditreport.com

Total estimate$1,400-$2,900

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. For 2026, 150% of poverty for a single person is approximately $22,590. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$3,000-$5,000Typical Houston range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Total estimate$3,400-$5,400Most paid through plan, not upfront

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

A major advantage of Chapter 13 is that attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment (often $0-$500), with the rest paid through your monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Lone Star Legal Aid - provides free legal services for qualifying low-income individuals

Fee waiver - eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments - pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing - filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended (10.8% of S.D. Tex. filers do this)

Reduced-fee credit counseling - many approved agencies offer reduced fees for low-income filers

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Houston?

The filing fee is $338. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000-$2,500. Total is typically $1,400-$2,900. Fee waivers are available for low-income filers. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Houston?

The filing fee is $313 and attorney fees typically range from $3,000-$5,000. Most costs can be paid through your repayment plan.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of poverty. Lone Star Legal Aid may provide free representation.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, fees are typically included in your repayment plan.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

## Bankruptcy by County - Houston Metro Area
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Counties

Bankruptcy by County - Houston Metro Area

Your county determines which federal court handles your bankruptcy case and which state exemptions protect your property. Find your county below for specific information.

5 Counties in the Houston Metro

Harris County

Harris County is the most populous county in Texas and the core of the Houston metro area. As the county seat of Houston, it handles the largest volume of bankruptcy filings in the Southern District of Texas.

District: Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

Harris County bankruptcy guide →

Fort Bend County

Fort Bend County is one of the fastest-growing counties in Texas, located southwest of Houston. Residents file bankruptcy in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division.

District: Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

Fort Bend County bankruptcy guide →

Montgomery County

Montgomery County is located north of Houston and includes The Woodlands and Conroe. Bankruptcy cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division.

District: Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

Montgomery County bankruptcy guide →

Brazoria County

Brazoria County is located south of Houston along the Gulf Coast. Unlike the other Houston metro counties, Brazoria County falls under the Galveston Division of the Southern District of Texas.

District: Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division)

Courthouse: 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550

Brazoria County bankruptcy guide →

Galveston County

Galveston County sits on the Gulf Coast southeast of Houston. It is part of the Galveston Division of the Southern District of Texas and has its own federal courthouse.

District: Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division)

Courthouse: 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550

Galveston County bankruptcy guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Which counties are in the Houston bankruptcy metro area?

The Houston metro area covers 5 counties: Harris County, Fort Bend County, Montgomery County, Brazoria County, Galveston County. Each county falls under a specific federal bankruptcy court district.

Does it matter which county I live in for bankruptcy?

Yes. Your county determines which federal district court handles your case, and potentially which state exemptions apply. Different districts may have different local rules, trustees, and dismissal rates.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Brazoria County - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/brazoria-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Brazoria County

Bankruptcy in Brazoria County

Brazoria County is located south of Houston along the Gulf Coast. Unlike the other Houston metro counties, Brazoria County falls under the Galveston Division of the Southern District of Texas.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Brazoria County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Brazoria County

Texas state exemptions apply in Brazoria County. Homestead: unlimited value, 10 acres urban / 100 acres rural. Personal property: up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family). One vehicle per licensed driver. Federal exemptions are not available in Texas.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Brazoria County

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which court division handles Brazoria County bankruptcies?

Brazoria County cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Galveston Division. The courthouse is at 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550. This is different from other Houston metro counties which use the Houston Division.

Do the same exemptions apply in Brazoria County as in Houston?

Yes. Texas state exemptions apply uniformly across all counties. The homestead exemption is unlimited in value (10 acres urban, 100 acres rural). Personal property exemptions total up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family).

How far is the Galveston courthouse from Brazoria County?

The Galveston Division courthouse is approximately 30-50 miles from most Brazoria County cities. Pearland residents are about 45 minutes away; Angleton residents are about 40 minutes away. Some hearings may be conducted by phone or video.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Other Houston Area Counties

Harris County
Fort Bend County
Montgomery County
Galveston County
All Counties →

## Bankruptcy in Fort Bend County - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/fort-bend-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Fort Bend County

Bankruptcy in Fort Bend County

Fort Bend County is one of the fastest-growing counties in Texas, located southwest of Houston. Residents file bankruptcy in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Fort Bend County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Fort Bend County

Texas state exemptions apply in Fort Bend County. The homestead exemption is unlimited in value for urban properties up to 10 acres (100 acres rural). Personal property exemptions total up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family). Each licensed household member can exempt one motor vehicle. Texas does not allow the use of federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Fort Bend County

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Houston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Fort Bend County cases?

Fort Bend County cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division, at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. Fort Bend County residents travel to the Houston courthouse for their 341 meeting of creditors and any court hearings.

What is the homestead exemption in Fort Bend County?

Texas has one of the most generous homestead exemptions in the nation - unlimited in value. For urban properties (which includes most of Fort Bend County), the acreage limit is 10 acres. This means most Fort Bend homeowners can protect their home equity fully in bankruptcy.

Is Fort Bend County considered urban or rural for exemptions?

Most of Fort Bend County is classified as urban for Texas exemption purposes, which means the 10-acre homestead limit applies. Some outlying areas may qualify as rural (100-acre limit). The classification depends on the specific location of your property.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Montgomery County
Brazoria County
Galveston County

## Bankruptcy in Galveston County - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/galveston-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Galveston County

Bankruptcy in Galveston County

Galveston County sits on the Gulf Coast southeast of Houston. It is part of the Galveston Division of the Southern District of Texas and has its own federal courthouse.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Galveston County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Galveston County

Texas state exemptions apply. Homestead: unlimited in value, 10 acres urban / 100 acres rural. Personal property: $50,000 (single) / $100,000 (family). One motor vehicle per licensed household member. Federal exemptions not available in Texas.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Galveston County

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Galveston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the bankruptcy courthouse for Galveston County?

Galveston County residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Galveston Division. The courthouse is conveniently located at 601 Rosenberg St, Galveston, TX 77550, within the county itself.

What are the filing fees for bankruptcy in Galveston County?

Filing fees are the same nationwide: $338 for Chapter 7 and $313 for Chapter 13. Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Chapter 13 filing fees can be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I protect my boat in Galveston County bankruptcy?

Texas exemptions do not specifically exempt boats. However, the generous personal property exemption ($50,000 single / $100,000 family) may cover a boat if you have room within your aggregate exemption. Consult a qualified attorney for asset-specific advice.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Harris County - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/harris-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Harris County

Bankruptcy in Harris County

Harris County is the most populous county in Texas and the core of the Houston metro area. As the county seat of Houston, it handles the largest volume of bankruptcy filings in the Southern District of Texas.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Harris County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Harris County

Texas offers some of the most generous bankruptcy exemptions in the country. The homestead exemption is unlimited in acreage for urban properties up to 10 acres (or 100 acres for rural). Personal property exemptions include up to $50,000 for a single filer or $100,000 for a family in aggregate personal property. Vehicle exemption: one motor vehicle per licensed household member. Texas does not allow federal exemptions - you must use the state exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Harris County

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Houston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which bankruptcy court handles Harris County cases?

Harris County bankruptcy cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The courthouse is located at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. Both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 cases for Harris County residents are handled at this location.

What exemptions apply in Harris County?

Texas state exemptions apply. The homestead exemption is unlimited in value for urban properties up to 10 acres. Personal property exemptions total up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family). Each licensed driver can exempt one motor vehicle.

How many bankruptcy cases are filed in Harris County?

Harris County consistently has the highest volume of bankruptcy filings in the Houston metro area, reflecting its large population of over 4.7 million residents. The Southern District of Texas - Houston Division handles thousands of cases annually.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Montgomery County
Brazoria County
Galveston County

## Bankruptcy in Montgomery County - Houston Bankruptcy Guide
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/counties/montgomery-county.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCountiesFAQOBP

Home » Counties » Montgomery County

Bankruptcy in Montgomery County

Montgomery County is located north of Houston and includes The Woodlands and Conroe. Bankruptcy cases are filed in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division.

Key Information

Federal District

Southern District of Texas (Houston Division)

All bankruptcy cases from Montgomery County are filed in this district.

Nearest Courthouse

515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002

341 meetings and court hearings are typically held at this location.

Texas Exemptions in Montgomery County

Texas state exemptions apply. Homestead exemption: unlimited value, up to 10 acres urban or 100 acres rural. Personal property: up to $50,000 (single) or $100,000 (family). One motor vehicle per licensed household member. Texas does not permit federal exemptions.

Exemptions determine what property you can keep in bankruptcy. See the full exemptions guide.

Filing Bankruptcy in Montgomery County

The Process

Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing ($15-25).

File your petition - The petition is filed with the Southern District of Texas (Houston Division). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

341 meeting - About 30 days after filing, attend a brief meeting of creditors at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002.

Debtor education - Complete a second financial course before discharge ($10-25).

Discharge - Chapter 7: approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Montgomery County residents file bankruptcy?

Montgomery County residents file in the Southern District of Texas, Houston Division. The courthouse is at 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. Your 341 meeting of creditors will typically be held in Houston.

Can I keep my home in Montgomery County if I file bankruptcy?

Texas offers an unlimited homestead exemption in value. If your Montgomery County property is 10 acres or less (urban) or 100 acres or less (rural), your home equity is fully protected. Many Montgomery County properties may qualify for the rural 100-acre exemption depending on location.

What is the median income for the means test in Montgomery County?

The means test uses Texas state median income figures, not county-specific numbers. For a household of one, the Texas median is approximately $59,000. Households above the median must pass additional calculations to qualify for Chapter 7.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Houston Federal Bankruptcy Court Guide - S.D. Texas Filing Information [2026]
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/court-guide.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Court Guide

Houston Federal Bankruptcy Court Guide

Everything you need to know about the United States Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of Texas. Address, filing hours, local rules, trustee panel, and pro se resources.

Court Location and Contact

United States Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of Texas

Bob Casey U.S. Courthouse, 515 Rusk Ave, Suite 6000, Houston, TX 77002

Phone: (713) 250-5500

Website: https://www.txsb.uscourts.gov

CM/ECF: https://ecf.txsb.uscourts.gov

PACER: pacer.uscourts.gov

Filing Hours and Procedures

Filing Hours: Monday-Friday, 8:00 AM - 4:00 PM CT. Electronic filing (CM/ECF) available 24/7.

Filing Fees: Chapter 7: $338 | Chapter 13: $313 | Chapter 11: $1,738

Fee Waivers: Available for Chapter 7 filers with income below 150% of the federal poverty line. Chapter 13 filers may request installment payments.

Electronic Filing: Attorneys must file through CM/ECF. Pro se debtors may file on paper at the clerk's window or request CM/ECF access.

Local Rules: https://www.txsb.uscourts.gov/local-rules

Trustee Panel Information

Chapter 7 trustees are assigned from the U.S. Trustee panel for Region 7. Standing Chapter 13 Trustees serve the Houston Division.

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases and maintains the panel of private trustees. You can find the current trustee panel at justice.gov/ust.

341 Meeting of Creditors

Location: 341 meetings for the Houston Division are held at the Bob Casey U.S. Courthouse, 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002, or at designated trustee meeting facilities, or by phone/video.

What to Bring: Government-issued photo ID, proof of Social Security number, most recent tax return, recent pay stubs, and recent bank statements.

Timing: The 341 meeting is typically scheduled 20-40 days after filing. You will receive a notice with the exact date, time, and format. For more: 341meeting.org.

Pro Se Resources

The S.D. Texas provides pro se resources through the clerk's office. Lone Star Legal Aid and Houston Volunteer Lawyers may provide free assistance.

Required Forms: All bankruptcy forms are available at uscourts.gov/forms/bankruptcy-forms.

Credit Counseling: You must complete credit counseling from an approved agency before filing. List of approved agencies: justice.gov/ust.

For more pro se filing resources: prosebankruptcy.org | filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com.

Local Filing Statistics

30,000+

Total Cases in FJC Data

38%

Chapter 7 Filings

61%

Chapter 13 Filings

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

FJC data covering S.D. Texas filings. Source: Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database. Attorney fee ranges are estimates based on local market data.

Exemptions: Texas has extremely generous exemptions. The homestead exemption protects your primary residence with no cap on value (up to 10 acres urban, 100/200 acres rural). Texas uses state exemptions under Tex. Prop. Code sec. 41 and sec. 42.

For detailed exemption information: bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Houston?

File at the Bob Casey U.S. Courthouse, 515 Rusk Ave, Houston, TX 77002. Complete credit counseling first, prepare your petition and schedules, and pay the filing fee. Attorneys must file electronically through CM/ECF.

What are the filing fees in the Southern District of Texas?

Chapter 7 costs $338 and Chapter 13 costs $313. You can pay in installments or apply for a fee waiver. Attorney fees in Houston typically range from $1,000-$2,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13.

Why is the Chapter 13 rate so high in Houston?

The S.D. Texas has a Chapter 13 filing rate above 60%, well above the national average. This is driven by local attorney practices favoring Chapter 13 for homeowners, strong trustee administration, and Texas exemption laws.

Is the S.D. Texas the same as the court for big corporate bankruptcies?

Yes. The S.D. Texas, particularly the Houston Division, has become one of the top venues for large Chapter 11 corporate bankruptcies. However, the vast majority of cases filed are consumer Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 cases.

Where are 341 meetings held in Houston?

341 meetings for the Houston Division are held at the Bob Casey U.S. Courthouse, 515 Rusk Ave, or at designated trustee meeting facilities, or by phone/video. Your notice specifies the exact format, date, and time.

## Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/credit-after-bankruptcy.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Credit After Bankruptcy

Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Houston

Bankruptcy is not the end of your financial life - it is a fresh start. Here is a practical timeline and action plan for Houston residents to rebuild credit after bankruptcy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Your Credit Score After Bankruptcy

Most people see a significant drop in their credit score when bankruptcy is filed, but the actual impact depends on where your score was before filing. Many filers report scores in the 500-550 range immediately after discharge.

The good news: your score will start climbing immediately if you take the right steps. Most Houston filers can reach the 650-700 range within 18-24 months of discharge.

10 yrs

Ch. 7 on credit report

7 yrs

Ch. 13 on credit report

18 mo

Typical time to 650+ score

2 yrs

FHA mortgage eligible

Step-by-Step Credit Rebuilding Plan

1Get a secured credit card (Month 1)

Deposit $200-500 which becomes your credit limit. Use for small purchases and pay the full balance monthly. Texas Dow Employees Credit Union (TDECU), JSC Federal Credit Union, Houston Federal Credit Union all offer secured cards.

2Become an authorized user (Month 1-3)

Ask a family member with good credit to add you as an authorized user. Their positive payment history will appear on your credit report.

3Get a credit-builder loan (Month 3-6)

The bank holds the loan amount in savings while you make payments. After payoff, you get the money. This adds an installment loan to your credit mix.

4Monitor your credit reports (Ongoing)

Check all three bureaus regularly. Dispute any debts that should show as discharged but still appear active.

5Apply for an unsecured card (Month 12-18)

After 12-18 months of on-time payments, you should qualify for an unsecured card. Some issuers auto-graduate secured cards.

Major Purchase Timeline After Bankruptcy

PurchaseAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

Credit cardImmediatelyImmediately

Car loanImmediatelyImmediately (with court approval)

FHA mortgage2 years after discharge1 year into plan (with approval)

Conventional mortgage4 years after discharge2 years after discharge

VA mortgage2 years after discharge1 year after discharge

USDA mortgage3 years after discharge1 year after discharge

Local Resources for Houston Residents

Credit Unions

Texas Dow Employees Credit Union (TDECU), JSC Federal Credit Union, Houston Federal Credit Union are excellent options for post-bankruptcy banking and credit rebuilding. Credit unions are member-owned and often more willing to work with people rebuilding after bankruptcy.

Legal Aid

If you need help with credit report disputes or post-bankruptcy issues: Lone Star Legal Aid (1-800-733-8394), Houston Volunteer Lawyers (713-228-0732).

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report in Texas?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, the impact on your score diminishes significantly after 2-3 years with active rebuilding.

Can I get a credit card after bankruptcy in Houston?

Yes. Many people receive offers within weeks of discharge. A secured credit card from Texas Dow Employees Credit Union (TDECU) is the best way to start rebuilding.

When can I buy a house after bankruptcy?

FHA loans are available 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan. Conventional loans require a 4-year wait after Chapter 7 or 2 years after Chapter 13 discharge. VA loans require 2 years after Chapter 7.

When can I buy a car after bankruptcy?

You can buy a car immediately after filing. Interest rates start higher (10-20%) but drop significantly as you rebuild credit. Many lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy auto financing.

How fast can I rebuild my credit score?

Most people reach 650-700 within 18-24 months of discharge by using a secured credit card responsibly, making all payments on time, and keeping utilization below 30%.

Does Texas have any special credit protection laws?

Texas's debtor-friendly exemption laws mean many filers emerge from bankruptcy with significant assets intact, giving them a stronger foundation for rebuilding credit. Houston has numerous credit unions like TDECU and JSC FCU that offer secured credit cards and credit-builder loans to post-bankruptcy filers.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/credit-card-debt.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Credit Card Debt

Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Houston

Credit card debt is the most common type of unsecured debt discharged in bankruptcy. For Houston residents, both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 can eliminate or restructure credit card balances.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Bankruptcy Eliminates Credit Card Debt

Chapter 7

Credit card debt is unsecured and generally dischargeable in Chapter 7. Most Houston filers receive a complete discharge of credit card balances within 3-4 months of filing. The debt is permanently eliminated through the discharge injunction.

Chapter 13

In Chapter 13, credit card debt is paid through your 3-5 year repayment plan based on your disposable income. Unsecured creditors often receive only a fraction of what is owed - sometimes as little as 0%. The remaining balance is discharged at plan completion.

When Credit Card Debt Is NOT Dischargeable

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2), credit card debt incurred through fraud is not dischargeable. The presumption of fraud applies to:

Luxury purchases over $800 made within 90 days of filing (single creditor)

Cash advances over $1,100 taken within 70 days of filing

Charges made with no intent to repay - using cards when you know you cannot pay

Materially false credit applications - lying about income or debts

These are rebuttable presumptions. You can overcome them by showing you did intend to repay or that the purchases were necessities, not luxuries.

Strategies for Houston Filers

Stop paying credit cards strategically. Once you decide to file, continuing minimum payments depletes funds you will need for the filing fee and attorney costs. However, get legal advice before stopping payments.

Do not transfer balances before filing. Balance transfers within 90 days can be challenged as preferential transfers.

Stop using credit cards once you decide to file. Charges made after the decision to file are more likely to be found non-dischargeable.

Texas exemptions protect your assets. Houston residents benefit from a unlimited (up to 10 acres urban, 100 acres rural) homestead exemption, keeping your home safe while discharging unsecured credit card debt.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge credit card debt in bankruptcy in Houston?

Yes. Credit card debt is unsecured and generally fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy. Most Houston filers in the Southern District of Texas eliminate their credit card balances completely.

What if I used my credit card right before filing?

Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days or cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are presumed fraudulent and may not be dischargeable. Wait at least 90 days after your last significant charge before filing.

Will I lose my credit cards if I file bankruptcy?

Yes. All credit card accounts included in your bankruptcy will be closed. Credit card companies typically close accounts even if you are current. You can begin rebuilding credit with secured cards after discharge.

Can creditors sue me for credit card debt?

Yes, but filing bankruptcy stops any lawsuit through the automatic stay. Texas does not allow consumer wage garnishment - one of only four states with this protection. Only child support, taxes, and student loans can be garnished. Filing before a judgment is entered is generally preferable.

How much credit card debt do I need to file bankruptcy?

There is no minimum debt requirement. However, the costs of filing (court fees plus attorney fees) should be weighed against the amount of debt. For small amounts, negotiation or debt management may be more practical.

Will bankruptcy stop credit card collection calls?

Yes. The automatic stay stops all collection activity the moment you file, including phone calls, letters, lawsuits, and garnishment. Violations can result in damages under section 362(k).

Ready to Explore Your Options?

Chapter 7 vs 13
Free Discharge Screener

## Using Credit Cards Before Filing Bankruptcy - Houston
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/credit-cards-before-filing.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13FAQOBP

Home›Using Credit Cards Before Filing Bankruptcy

Using Credit Cards Before Filing Bankruptcy

A free guide for Texas residents considering bankruptcy. Filed in S.D. Tex..

What You Need to Know

Using credit cards when you know you will file bankruptcy can be considered fraud. The Bankruptcy Code presumes fraud for luxury goods purchases over $725 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,000 within 70 days of filing. Best practice: stop using all credit cards immediately once you decide to file. The creditor can challenge discharge of those specific charges.

Filed in S.D. Tex.. Courthouse: 515 Rusk Ave, Houston TX.

Frequently Asked Questions

Using Credit Cards Before Filing Bankruptcy

Using credit cards when you know you will file bankruptcy can be considered fraud. The Bankruptcy Code presumes fraud for luxury goods purchases over $725 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,000 within 70 days of filing. Best practice: stop using all credit cards immediately once you decide to file. The creditor can challenge discharge of those specific charges.

Does this apply in Texas?

Yes. This applies to S.D. Tex.. Local trustee practices may vary. Consult a qualified attorney.

Where is the courthouse?

515 Rusk Ave, Houston TX.

## Types of Debt in Houston Bankruptcy
Source: https://houstonbankruptcy.org/debt-types.html

Houston Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostCountiesFAQOBP

Home›Houston Bankruptcy Guide›Debt Types

Types of Debt in Houston Bankruptcy

Not all debts are treated the same in bankruptcy. Here is a complete guide to which debts can be eliminated and which survive for Houston residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Dischargeable Debts

Credit card debt: 100% dischargeable (minor exceptions for recent luxury purchases)

Medical bills: 100% dischargeable - the #1 cause of bankruptcy

Personal loans: Dischargeable including loans from friends and family

Utility bills: Past-due amounts dischargeable

Payday loans: Fully dischargeable

Lawsuit judgments: Most money judgments dischargeable

Repossession deficiency: Remaining balance after repo and sale

Business debts: Personally guaranteed debts dischargeable

Old tax debt: Federal income taxes meeting the 3-2-240 rule

Non-Dischargeable Debts

Child support and alimony: Never dischargeable

Student loans: Presumed non-dischargeable unless undue hardship proven

Recent tax debt: Taxes not meeting 3-2-240 rules

Debts from fraud: False pretenses, false representation

DUI injury debts: Death or personal injury from drunk driving

Criminal fines: Court-ordered criminal penalties

Government penalties: Non-compensatory fines

Secured vs. Unsecured Debt in Texas

Secured Debts

Backed by collateral (mortgage, car loan). You must return the collateral or continue paying. Texas exemptions protect: Unlimited (10 acres urban) homestead, Fully exempt if needed for work vehicle.

Unsecured Debts

No collateral (credit cards, medical bills). Typically eliminated entirely in Chapter 7, or paid pennies on the dollar in Chapter 13.

Priority Debts

Domestic support obligations (child support, alimony) - highest priority

Administrative expenses (trustee fees, attorney fees)

Priority tax claims (recent income taxes, payroll taxes)

Employee wages within 180 days before filing (up to $15,150 per employee)

In Chapter 13, all priority debts must be paid in full through your plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts can be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most unsecured debts: credit card debt, medical bills, personal loans, utility bills, lease obligations, payday loans, and most lawsuit judgments.

What debts cannot be discharged?

Child support/alimony, most student loans, recent tax debts, debts from fraud, DUI injury judgments, criminal fines, and government penalties.

Is credit card debt dischargeable?

Yes. 100% dischargeable. However, luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days or cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days may be challenged.

Can I discharge a car loan in Houston?

If you surrender the vehicle, the remaining balance is dischargeable. If you keep the car, you continue paying. Texas's vehicle exemption of Fully exempt if needed for work protects your equity.

What about payday loans?

Payday loans are generally dischargeable. Courts consistently reject lender arguments that they are non-dischargeable.

Are personal loans from family dischargeable?

Yes, but payments to family within one year of filing may be recovered by the trustee as preferential transfers.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
